title: How anxiety affects the body
citation: Barlow, D.H. (2002). Anxiety and its disorders: The nature and treatment of anxiety and panic (2nd ed.). Guilford Press.
content:
When we feel anxious, the body does not stay passive. The physical symptoms of anxiety are real, sometimes intense, and can be frightening precisely because they feel so bodily. Understanding where these sensations come from can help make them less alarming.

At the centre of the physical anxiety response is the fight-or-flight system, a survival mechanism that evolved to prepare the body for immediate danger. When the brain perceives a threat, it sends a signal that triggers the release of stress hormones, including adrenaline and cortisol. These hormones cause a cascade of physical changes designed to help you fight or flee. The heart beats faster to pump more blood to the muscles. Breathing quickens to take in more oxygen. Digestion slows, which can cause nausea, stomach discomfort, or that familiar sinking sensation in the gut. Muscles tense, ready for action, which over time can cause aching, tightness in the chest, or headaches.

Other common physical symptoms of anxiety include dizziness or lightheadedness, often caused by slight changes in breathing patterns; tingling or numbness in the hands and feet; sweating; dry mouth; and a feeling of unreality or detachment from the body. None of these sensations are dangerous, even when they feel alarming. They are the result of a system that is working exactly as designed, responding vigorously to a signal that may or may not reflect a genuine external threat.

One of the difficulties with anxiety is that the physical symptoms can themselves become a source of fear. Someone who notices their heart beating quickly may interpret this as a sign that something is medically wrong, which increases anxiety, which increases the heart rate further, creating a cycle. Breaking this cycle often involves understanding that the physical sensations of anxiety, however unpleasant, are time-limited, benign, and a product of the nervous system doing its job.
